paper.title: Dietary fiber-induced Faecalibacterium prausnitzii expansion attenuates colitis through butyrate-mediated IL-10 induction in mucosal macrophages
paper.abstract: Dietary fiber fermentation by Faecalibacterium prausnitzii produces butyrate that induces anti-inflammatory IL-10 secretion in intestinal macrophages. In a randomized controlled trial of 120 IBD patients, high-fiber diet (30g/day) over 12 weeks significantly expanded F. prausnitzii abundance (p<0.001), reduced fecal calprotectin by 42%, and elevated mucosal IL-10 levels. Butyrate enema controls confirmed the mechanistic link. These findings support dietary fiber as a microbiome-mediated immunomodulatory intervention in IBD.

classification.label: ANSWERS
classification.reason: Directly addresses oq_1 by demonstrating the fiber→F. prausnitzii→butyrate→IL-10 mechanism linking dietary fiber, microbiome composition, and systemic immune modulation in IBD patients.

paper.title: Akkermansia muciniphila outer membrane protein Amuc_1100 directly binds TLR2 to reduce intestinal permeability independent of bacterial viability
paper.abstract: The beneficial metabolic effects of Akkermansia muciniphila have been attributed to its outer membrane protein Amuc_1100. Here we show Amuc_1100 binds TLR2 on intestinal epithelial cells, activating tight junction protein expression (claudin-3, occludin) and reducing permeability in a viability-independent manner. Amuc_1100 administration to diet-induced obese mice reduced plasma LPS by 55%, improved glucose tolerance (p<0.01), and decreased adipose tissue inflammation, confirming that pasteurization preserves therapeutic activity through this protein-receptor mechanism.

classification.label: EXTENDS
classification.reason: Extends fnd_3 by providing the molecular mechanism (Amuc_1100/TLR2 binding and tight junction upregulation) explaining why pasteurized Akkermansia retains metabolic efficacy without viable bacteria.

paper.title: Tryptophan catabolism by Lactobacillus reuteri generates indole-3-aldehyde that suppresses neuroinflammation via aryl hydrocarbon receptor signaling in microglia
paper.abstract: Gut-derived tryptophan metabolites influence neuroinflammation through the gut-brain axis. We identified that Lactobacillus reuteri converts dietary tryptophan to indole-3-aldehyde (IAld), which crosses the blood-brain barrier and activates aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) in microglia, suppressing LPS-induced neuroinflammation by 67% in ex vivo models. Germ-free mice colonized with L. reuteri showed reduced hippocampal IL-6 and TNF-α, with spatial memory improvements in Morris water maze. IAld serum levels inversely correlated with neuroinflammation markers in 85 human subjects.

classification.label: ANSWERS
classification.reason: Directly addresses oq_2 by identifying a specific tryptophan metabolite (IAld) and its AhR-mediated neuroimmune signaling pathway linking gut microbiota to neuroinflammation and cognition.

paper.title: Causal inference from observational microbiome cohorts: when does Firmicutes/Bacteroidetes ratio predict metabolic outcomes?
paper.abstract: The Firmicutes-to-Bacteroidetes (F/B) ratio has been widely cited as a biomarker of obesity and metabolic syndrome, but causal evidence remains contested. Using Mendelian randomization across 12 population cohorts (n=47,000), we find that F/B ratio is associated with BMI (β=0.14, p=2×10⁻⁸) but not with type 2 diabetes incidence after controlling for dietary confounders. Longitudinal analysis shows diet explains 78% of F/B variance, suggesting ratio changes are a consequence rather than cause of dietary patterns. These findings challenge asm_2-type assumptions about F/B as a causal driver of metabolic disease.

classification.label: CONTRADICTS
classification.reason: Contradicts asm_2 by showing via Mendelian randomization that the F/B ratio is a consequence of diet rather than a causal driver of metabolic disease in a large multi-cohort analysis.

paper.title: Roseburia inulinivorans inulin fermentation upregulates colonic epithelial autophagy through propionate-HDAC inhibition
paper.abstract: Roseburia inulinivorans, a major inulin-fermenting taxon, produces propionate that inhibits histone deacetylases (HDACs) in colonocytes, upregulating autophagy genes (BECN1, ATG5, ATG7) by 2.8-fold. In a fiber supplementation cohort (n=45), R. inulinivorans expansion correlated with propionate levels (r=0.71) and reduced markers of cellular senescence in colonic biopsies. HDAC inhibitor treatment phenocopied these effects. These findings reveal a new mechanism connecting dietary fiber fermentation to colonic epithelial health maintenance.

classification.label: EXTENDS
classification.reason: Extends fnd_1 by revealing a propionate/HDAC-inhibition/autophagy mechanism through which Roseburia inulinivorans fiber fermentation maintains colonic epithelial health beyond SCFA production alone.
